home.title: share.env  Secure .env File Sharing for Teams
home.description: Share .env files and API keys with your team securely  no more pasting secrets in Slack. AES-256 encryption, role-based access, and expiring links. Free for up to 3 environments.

billing.title: Billing

featuresPage.title: Secure .env File Sharing: Everything You Need to Keep Secrets Safe
featuresPage.description: AES-256 encryption, role-based access control, and full audit history built into every workspace  not bolted on afterwards.

securityDeepDive.heading: Security enforced at the database, not the UI
securityDeepDive.subheading: Every table in share.env has Row Level Security turned on. A hidden button in the interface is not a permission boundary. A policy is.

items.title: Row Level Security on every table
items.description: Postgres policies decide who can read or write a row before your application code ever runs, so a bug in the UI can't leak another team's secrets.

items.title: AES-256 encryption at rest
items.description: File contents are encrypted in Supabase Storage. Nobody, including us, reads a .env file outside of an authorized request.

items.title: High-entropy share tokens
items.description: Links are generated with cryptographically secure tokens, always carry an expiry date, and can be revoked the moment access should end.

items.title: Full audit trail
items.description: Every change to a file is recorded with who made it and when, so security reviews take minutes instead of days.

comparison.heading: Stop pasting secrets into chat
comparison.subheading: The way most teams share environment variables today creates a permanent, unencrypted, unrevokable copy of your secrets.
comparison.beforeLabel: Slack, email, or a shared doc
before: API keys sit in searchable chat history forever
Anyone in the channel can read them, no access control
No way to know who copied a secret or when
Rotating a leaked key means guessing who still has it
after: Files live encrypted in a workspace, never in a chat log
Owner, editor, and viewer roles enforced by the database
Every access and change is recorded with a timestamp
Revoke a link or a member's access in a single click

integrations.heading: Fits into the pipeline you already have
integrations.subheading: Pull the current environment straight into local development or a CI/CD run, without anyone copying secrets by hand.
items: Official CLI for local development and scripts
Drop-in step for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Vercel
Scoped tokens per project, never your personal login
Every pull is logged like any other file access

useCasesPage.title: The moments your secrets actually change hands
useCasesPage.description: Onboarding, key rotation, and offboarding are where .env files leak. Here's why teams switch, and how each moment actually works.

cliPage.title: Pull secrets without leaving the terminal
cliPage.description: envshare is a small command-line tool that clones the repo behind an environment and writes its .env for you, respecting the same password, 2FA, and access-key protection as the web app.

overview.heading: One login, then it stays out of your way
overview.subheading: Authenticate once with a device code, the same flow GitHub's CLI uses. From there, every pull is a single command.
items: Device-code login, no password typed into the terminal
Protected environments prompt for password and 2FA inline
Writes straight to .env with owner-only file permissions

useCases.heading: Where it actually gets used
useCases.subheading: Three moments where a terminal command beats opening the browser.

useCaseItems.title: Onboarding a new teammate
useCaseItems.description: They run one command, confirm a code in the browser once, and the repo plus its .env are both sitting in their working directory.
useCaseItems.payoff: No more "can you send me the env file" in a DM.

useCaseItems.title: Daily local development
useCaseItems.description: Already have the repo cloned? envshare pull refreshes .env from whatever the workspace currently holds, in place.
useCaseItems.payoff: The .env in your editor matches what's actually deployed.

useCaseItems.title: Rotating a leaked key
useCaseItems.description: Update the value once in the workspace, then everyone re-runs envshare pull instead of passing the new key around by hand.
useCaseItems.payoff: The exposure window is one pull, not one thread.

install.heading: Install it
install.subheading: One package, no separate download. Run it with npx, or install it once if you'll use it daily.
steps: Run it directly with npx, nothing to install
Or install it once for the plain envshare command
Log in and it's ready in any repo
install.code:
npx @kpz231/envshare login

# or install once:
npm install -g @kpz231/envshare
envshare login

commands.heading: Command reference
commands.subheading: Five commands cover the whole workflow.

items.name: envshare login
items.description: Starts a device-code login and saves your CLI session locally.

items.name: envshare list
items.description: Lists every environment your account can access, by workspace.

items.name: envshare clone <name>
items.description: Clones the linked repo and writes its .env, prompting for a password or 2FA code if the environment is protected.

items.name: envshare pull
items.description: Re-downloads .env into the current directory, for repos already cloned.

items.name: envshare logout
items.description: Revokes the local session and forgets the saved token.

privacyPage.title: Privacy Policy
privacyPage.intro: This policy explains what personal data share.env collects, why, and what rights you have over it. It does not cover the contents of the .env files you store  those are your data, encrypted at rest, and we only process them to provide the service you asked for.

sections.heading: 1. Who is responsible for your data
paragraphs: share.env is operated by KPZsProductions. For anything in this policy, or to exercise any of the rights below, contact us at kpzsproductionscontact@gmail.com.

sections.heading: 2. What we collect
paragraphs: We collect only what the product needs to function:
list: Account data: email address, and anything you choose to add to your profile (display name, bio, avatar).
Workspace data: workspace name, description, logo, membership roles, and invitations you send or receive.
The .env files and variables you upload  encrypted at rest, scoped to your workspace, never analyzed or read by us outside an authorized support request you initiate.
Security data needed to protect your account: password hashes, TOTP and passkey credentials, and access logs for protected environments.
Basic technical data: IP address and timestamps for security-relevant actions (sign-in, share link creation, environment downloads), kept only as long as needed for abuse prevention.

sections.heading: 3. Why we process it (legal basis)
list: Performance of a contract: creating your account, running your workspace, and delivering the file-sharing service you signed up for (GDPR Art. 6(1)(b)).
Legitimate interest: securing the service against abuse, debugging failures, and preventing unauthorized access (Art. 6(1)(f)).
Consent: optional product update emails and any analytics you explicitly opt into (Art. 6(1)(a))  you can withdraw this at any time from your profile settings.

sections.heading: 4. Who else processes your data
paragraphs: We use a small number of infrastructure providers to run the service, each acting as our data processor under a data processing agreement:
list: Supabase  database, authentication, and encrypted file storage.
Vercel  application hosting and edge network.
A transactional email provider  for invite emails and account notifications only.
We do not sell your data, and we do not share it with advertisers or data brokers.

sections.heading: 5. International transfers
paragraphs: Our infrastructure providers may process data outside the European Economic Area (for example, in the United States). Where that happens, transfers are covered by Standard Contractual Clauses or an equivalent safeguard recognized under GDPR Chapter V.

sections.heading: 6. How long we keep it
paragraphs: Account and workspace data is kept while your account is active. If you delete your account, your profile and any workspace you solely own are removed; environments are deleted with their storage objects. Security logs are kept for a limited period strictly for abuse investigation, then deleted.

sections.heading: 7. Your rights
paragraphs: Under GDPR and similar laws, you have the right to:
list: Access the personal data we hold about you (see the data export tool on your profile page).
Correct inaccurate data, or delete your account and associated data.
Object to or restrict certain processing, and withdraw consent where processing is based on it.
Receive your data in a portable format, and lodge a complaint with your local data protection authority (in Poland, the UODO).

sections.heading: 8. Additional rights for California residents (CCPA/CPRA)
paragraphs: If you are a California resident, you have the right to know what personal information we collect, request its deletion, and opt out of its sale. We do not sell personal information as defined by the CCPA, so there is nothing to opt out of.

sections.heading: 9. Security
paragraphs: Every table is protected by Row Level Security enforced at the database, not just the interface. File contents are encrypted at rest. Share links use high-entropy tokens, always expire, and can be revoked instantly. See our security page for details.

sections.heading: 10. Children
paragraphs: share.env is not directed at children under 16, and we do not knowingly collect their data.

sections.heading: 11. Changes to this policy
paragraphs: If we make a material change, we'll update the date at the top of this page and, where appropriate, notify account owners by email.

sections.heading: 12. Contact
paragraphs: Questions about this policy or your data: kpzsproductionscontact@gmail.com.

termsPage.title: Terms of Service
termsPage.intro: These terms govern your use of share.env. By creating an account, you agree to them. If you're accepting on behalf of a company, you confirm you have the authority to do so.

sections.heading: 1. The service
paragraphs: share.env lets a workspace store .env files and share them with teammates under owner, editor, and viewer roles, and generate expiring links for people without an account. We may add, change, or remove features at any time; we'll try to give notice before removing anything you actively rely on.

sections.heading: 2. Your account
list: You must provide a valid email address and keep your credentials confidential.
You're responsible for all activity under your account, including anything done by workspace members you invite.
You must be at least 16 years old to create an account.
Notify us immediately at kpzsproductionscontact@gmail.com if you suspect unauthorized access.

sections.heading: 3. Acceptable use
paragraphs: You agree not to use share.env to:
list: Store or share content you don't have the legal right to hold (e.g. someone else's credentials without authorization).
Attempt to bypass workspace roles, Row Level Security, or any other access control.
Probe, scan, or attack the service's infrastructure, or resell access without our written agreement.
Upload malware or content that violates applicable law.

sections.heading: 4. Your content, our license
paragraphs: You retain all rights to the .env files and other content you upload. You grant us a limited license to store, encrypt, back up, and transmit that content solely to operate the service for you  we don't use it for any other purpose, and we don't claim ownership of it.

sections.heading: 5. Plans and billing
paragraphs: The free plan covers up to 3 environments. Beyond that, environments are billed at $2 per environment per month, billed monthly. Downgrading or deleting environments takes effect at the next billing cycle; we don't provide partial-month refunds except where required by law.

sections.heading: 6. Availability
paragraphs: We aim for high availability but the service is provided "as is", without an uptime guarantee unless a separate written SLA says otherwise. Scheduled maintenance will be announced where practical.

sections.heading: 7. Limitation of liability
paragraphs: To the maximum extent permitted by law, share.env is not liable for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages, or for losses arising from your own mismanagement of credentials, workspace roles, or share links. Nothing in these terms limits liability that cannot be limited under applicable law.

sections.heading: 8. Suspension and termination
paragraphs: We may suspend or terminate an account that violates section 3, with notice where feasible. You can delete your account at any time from your profile page; workspaces you solely own are deleted along with their environments.

sections.heading: 9. Changes to these terms
paragraphs: We'll post material changes here with an updated date, and notify account owners by email for changes that meaningfully reduce your rights.

sections.heading: 10. Governing law
paragraphs: These terms are governed by the laws of Poland, without regard to conflict-of-law rules, without prejudice to any mandatory consumer-protection rights you have under the law of your country of residence.

sections.heading: 11. Contact
paragraphs: Questions about these terms: kpzsproductionscontact@gmail.com.

cookiesPage.title: Cookie Policy
cookiesPage.intro: share.env uses a short list of strictly necessary cookies to keep you signed in and remember your active workspace. We don't run analytics or advertising cookies, so there's nothing else to disclose here.

sections.heading: 1. What cookies we set
list: Supabase authentication cookies (names prefixed sb-)  keep you signed in between requests. Removed on sign-out or expiry.
active_workspace  remembers which workspace you last viewed, so the dashboard opens on the right one next time.

sections.heading: 2. Why no consent banner toggle
paragraphs: Both cookies above are strictly necessary to provide the service you're logged in to use  they don't track you across other sites, and they carry no advertising or analytics purpose. Under the ePrivacy rules most jurisdictions follow, strictly necessary cookies are exempt from opt-in consent; that's why our banner is a simple acknowledgement rather than a toggle list.

sections.heading: 3. Managing cookies
paragraphs: You can block or delete cookies from your browser settings at any time. Doing so will sign you out and reset your active workspace selection, since those cookies are what keep that state.

sections.heading: 4. Changes
paragraphs: If we ever add a non-essential cookie (analytics, for example), we'll update this page and add a real opt-in control before doing so.

sections.heading: 5. Contact
paragraphs: Questions about cookies: kpzsproductionscontact@gmail.com.

cookieConsent.message: We use only strictly necessary cookies to keep you signed in and remember your workspace. See our <link>Cookie Policy</link> for details.

securityPage.title: Built on access control, not obscurity
securityPage.description: Every table has Row Level Security. Every file is encrypted at rest. Here's the detail behind that, and how to report a problem if you find one.

sections.heading: Infrastructure and sub-processors
paragraphs: share.env runs on a small, named set of providers, each under a data processing agreement:
list: Supabase  Postgres database, authentication, and encrypted file storage. Row Level Security policies run inside the database itself, so authorization holds even if application code has a bug.
Vercel  application hosting and edge network for the web app and API routes.
A transactional email provider  used only for invite and account emails.

sections.heading: How access is enforced
list: Row Level Security is enabled on every table with no exceptions; the default is deny-all, and every operation needs an explicit policy.
Workspace roles (owner, editor, viewer) are enforced by those database policies, not just hidden in the UI  a missing button is UX, not a security boundary.
Share links use cryptographically generated, high-entropy tokens, always carry an expiry, and can be revoked instantly.
Password-protected environments layer a scrypt-hashed password with TOTP or a passkey, and optionally a workspace-issued Access Key, before any content is returned.

sections.heading: Data protection
paragraphs: File contents are encrypted at rest in Supabase Storage. We don't log the contents of your .env files or access tokens in application logs, error trackers, or analytics.

sections.heading: Compliance status
paragraphs: share.env is an early-stage product. We haven't pursued SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certification yet, and we won't claim we have. What we do have is enforced from day one: database-level RLS, encryption at rest, and least-privilege use of service-role credentials. We'll update this page if that changes.

sections.heading: Reporting a vulnerability
paragraphs: If you find a security issue, email kpzsproductionscontact@gmail.com with details and, if possible, steps to reproduce. We don't yet run a paid bug bounty program, but we will acknowledge reports, fix confirmed issues promptly, and credit reporters who want credit.
Please don't test against other users' data or workspaces you don't own; use your own account and environments.

useCasePersonas.heading: Built for how your team actually works
useCasePersonas.subheading: The right fit depends on how many people touch your secrets and how often that list changes.

items.title: Startup teams
items.description: A handful of engineers ship fast and the .env file changes every week. You need everyone on the same values without a Slack thread.
items.payoff: New hires get access in minutes, and nobody re-pastes a stale API key into their local setup again.

items.title: Agencies with client work
items.description: Each client gets a separate workspace with its own secrets, so a contractor rolling off one project never sees another client's keys.
items.payoff: Revoke a contractor's access the day the contract ends, without rotating every key by hand.

items.title: Platform and DevOps teams
items.description: Dozens of services, multiple environments, and a compliance review that asks who touched production secrets last quarter.
items.payoff: The audit trail already exists. Export it instead of reconstructing it from memory.

usageScenarios.heading: How the day-to-day actually looks
usageScenarios.subheading: Three moments where secrets usually leak, and what happens instead when they run through share.env.

items.title: Onboarding a new engineer
items.result: The new hire is unblocked the same day, and their access disappears automatically if they never accept the invite.
steps: Invite them to the workspace by email with a role: viewer to start, editor once they're ramped
They accept the invite and sign in, no shared password to hand off
They pull the current .env for their project with the CLI or copy it from the workspace

items.title: Rotating a leaked key
items.result: The exposure window is minutes, not the days it takes to track down every place a pasted key ended up.
steps: Revoke every active share link for the affected file immediately
Update the value in the workspace; the change is versioned automatically
Teammates and CI pull the new value on their next run, no manual re-share needed

items.title: Offboarding a contractor
items.result: Access ends the moment you remove them, not whenever someone remembers to rotate the shared keys.
steps: Remove their membership from the workspace or workspaces they had access to
Any link they generated is revoked along with their access
Check the audit log to confirm exactly what they touched while they had access

howItWorks.title: How to Share .env Files Securely: Four Steps
howItWorks.lead: No more pasting API keys and database passwords into Slack. Upload once, and share.env handles encryption, links, and access for you.

steps.title: Upload your .env file
steps.description: Drag in your environment file or paste its contents. It's stored in your workspace, not in a chat thread.

steps.title: Encrypted at rest
steps.description: Every secret is encrypted in Supabase Storage and protected by row-level security, so only authorized requests ever reach it.

steps.title: Generate a share link
steps.description: Create a link with a high-entropy token, an expiry date, and revoke it instantly whenever access should end.

steps.title: Team access by role
steps.description: Invite teammates by email or workspace role  owner, editor, or viewer  and permissions are enforced on every request.

features.heading: Secret security, built for teams
features.subheading: Every .env file lives in a team workspace, not on one person's laptop  with access control and version history from day one.

items.title: File encryption
items.description: Your .env files are encrypted at rest, and Postgres Row Level Security enforces who can even read them  at the database layer, not just the UI.

items.title: Expiring links
items.description: Share a file without inviting someone to the team  every link carries an expiry date and can be revoked instantly, at any time.

items.title: Per-team access control
items.description: Owner, editor, and viewer roles decide who can edit secrets and who can only view them  enforced by database policies, not just hidden in the UI.

items.title: Version history
items.description: Every change to a .env file is recorded, so you always know who changed which secret and when  and can roll back to a previous version.

items.title: CLI & CI/CD integration
items.description: Pull the current environment variables straight into your deployment pipeline, without copying secrets between environments by hand.

items.question: What counts as an environment?
items.answer: Any distinct .env file you upload to a workspace, for example production, staging, or a per-branch preview file. Re-uploading the same file doesn't create a second one.

items.question: Do the first 3 environments ever expire?
items.answer: No. The free tier is permanent for up to 3 environments, not a trial. You're only billed once you add a 4th.

items.question: Can I remove an environment to lower my bill?
items.answer: Yes. Deleting an environment removes it from that month's count immediately, and the next invoice reflects the change.

items.question: Is there a limit on team members or storage?
items.answer: No. Team members are unlimited on every plan; pricing scales only with the number of environments you store.

testimonials.heading: Trusted by the devs and CTOs who own security
testimonials.subheading: Technical people who decide where their team's secrets live, on what convinced them to switch to share.env.

items.quote: API keys used to float around in Slack threads. Now every secret has an owner, a version history, and a link I can revoke in one click  security audits stopped being a nightmare.
items.name: Marta Wojcik

items.quote: The real difference is RLS at the database layer, not just the UI. Even if someone bypassed the frontend, they can't pull another team's environment variables  that's the bar I expect from a secrets tool.
items.name: Adam Nowicki

items.quote: Rollout took an afternoon: CLI wired into CI/CD, owner/editor/viewer roles for the team, and no more copying .env files between environments by hand. Simple without giving up control.
items.name: Julia Kaminska

faq.heading: share.env FAQ: Security and Data Retention
faq.contactText: Send us a message  we usually reply to security and onboarding questions the same business day.

items.question: How exactly are .env files encrypted?
items.answer: Every .env file's content is encrypted with AES-256-GCM before it's ever written to storage  only the encrypted envelope (ciphertext, IV, and authentication tag) lands in Supabase Storage, never the plaintext secrets. GCM is authenticated encryption: any attempt to tamper with the encrypted data causes decryption to fail loudly instead of silently returning corrupted content. On top of that, Supabase Storage applies its own default encryption at rest, and Postgres Row Level Security ensures only queries authorized for your workspace can read the underlying records. As a result, even someone with direct access to the database or storage bucket can't read your secrets without the encryption key.

items.question: How long do you keep files and version history?
items.answer: Files and their version history are kept for as long as the workspace stays active. Deleting a workspace or a specific file also removes its stored versions and share links  we don't keep copies outside your account's active scope.

items.question: Can I see who had access to a given secret?
items.answer: Yes. Every change to a .env file is logged with who made it and when, so the version history doubles as an audit trail for the whole workspace. Token-based share links always carry a mandatory expiration date, and any owner or editor can revoke a link at any time from the dashboard  revocation takes effect immediately and blocks further access through that link, even if someone already opened it. The same applies to role-based access: removing someone from a workspace or downgrading them to viewer instantly limits what they can read or edit, because permissions are enforced by RLS policies rather than just hidden UI buttons.

items.question: Is self-hosting available?
items.answer: Right now share.env only runs as a hosted service  self-hosting isn't supported. The whole stack (database, auth, storage) runs on Supabase, with the application layer hosted on Vercel, which lets us maintain one consistently high security bar (RLS on every table and bucket, AES-256-GCM encryption of file contents, rate limiting on public endpoints) instead of pushing server patching and hardening onto you. We've also deliberately skipped client-side zero-knowledge end-to-end encryption in favor of this model  it keeps team sharing simple (owner/editor/viewer roles, token-based links) while still layering on the protections above. If self-hosting is a hard requirement for you, reach out directly  we treat that feedback as a signal for roadmap prioritization.

items.question: What happens to my secrets if I delete my account?
items.answer: Deleting your account permanently removes files you solely owned, along with their version history and active share links. Files belonging to workspaces with other members remain accessible to the remaining members with the right role.

settingsIntegrations.title: Integrations
settingsIntegrations.subtitle: Connect this workspace to the tools you already use, and push environment variables straight into them.
settingsIntegrations.ownerOnly: Only the workspace owner can manage integrations.
settingsIntegrations.connectGithubFirst: Connect GitHub in your profile first

docs.title: How this works
docs.intro: Connect a provider, pick which environment it pushes to, then use "Sync now" whenever you want to push the current values.

githubActions.title: GitHub Actions
githubActions.body: Connect GitHub in your profile first, then click "Use GitHub connection" here. Pick a repo and sync — variables land as encrypted repository secrets.

gitlab.title: GitLab
gitlab.body: Create a personal access token with the "api" scope, paste it here (add your self-hosted host if not gitlab.com), then pick a project to sync CI/CD variables into.

vercel.title: Vercel
vercel.body: Create an access token in Vercel's account settings, paste it here, then pick a project. Variables are added for production, preview, and development.

netlify.title: Netlify
netlify.body: Create a personal access token in Netlify's user settings, paste it here, then pick a site to sync environment variables into.

slack.title: Slack
slack.body: Add an Incoming Webhook to a Slack channel and paste its URL here. You'll get an alert when a share link is about to expire or a protected environment is rotated.

discord.title: Discord
discord.body: Create a webhook for a channel (Channel Settings → Integrations → Webhooks) and paste its URL here for the same expiry/rotation alerts.

wipNotice.title: Still a work in progress
wipNotice.body: Integrations are new and may not work perfectly yet — some providers or actions could fail unexpectedly.
wipNotice.reportPrefix: Found a bug? Report it to:
